lead_signal.headline: Russia's Moldova FIMI playbook is being actively redeployed against Armenia ahead of June 2026 parliamentary elections, per EEAS 4th FIMI Threat Report and live EUvsDisinfo monitoring.
lead_signal.confidence_basis: EEAS 4th FIMI Threat Report (March 2026) and EUvsDisinfo weekly monitoring (published ~April 28–May 4 window) both independently document tactical replication from Moldova 2025 to Armenia 2026, including network-level similarities. Attribution is multi-source (EEAS + EUvsDisinfo) and behaviour-based. No single-source dependency.
lead_signal.note: MF2 flagged at campaign level only: the EEAS report is the primary analytical anchor; EUvsDisinfo corroborates independently. Attribution to Russian state is assessed, not confirmed — behavioural and network evidence is strong but no formal government attribution statement was issued this week. The Armenia election FIMI warning is forward-looking; active incidents are documented but the full campaign is still developing.

campaigns.target: Armenia — June 2026 parliamentary elections; pro-European political leadership
campaigns.summary: Russian FIMI infrastructure previously deployed against Moldova's 2025 parliamentary elections is being redirected toward Armenia ahead of its June 2026 elections. Tactics include fear-spreading, smear campaigns against pro-European leaders, and framing EU-Armenia cooperation as 'foreign interference.' The EEAS identifies striking network and tactical similarities between the Moldova and Armenia operations.
campaigns.new_this_week: EUvsDisinfo published active monitoring content this week documenting the Armenia FIMI escalation, consistent with the EEAS 4th Threat Report's forward assessment. The narrative framing — portraying EU-Armenia ties as illegitimate foreign interference — is now actively circulating in the Armenian information environment.

sources_cited.excerpt: attack patterns in Moldova reveal striking similarities with networks and tactics now emerging in Armenia
Russia intensified FIMI efforts, using familiar tactics tested in Moldova: spreading fear, attacking pro-European leaders

search_queries_run: EU EUvsDisinfo FIMI state-sponsored disinformation May 2026
Meta CIB coordinated inauthentic behaviour report 2026
research_traceback.confidence_notes: Two independent EU-institutional sources (EEAS analytical report + EUvsDisinfo operational monitoring) converge on the same assessment. Attribution to Russia is behaviour-based and network-based, not solely content-based. No formal government attribution statement issued this week elevates this above Assessed to High.

campaigns.operation_name: EU Sanctions: Euromore and Pravfond Designations
campaigns.target: European audiences — EU legitimacy, Ukraine support narratives
campaigns.summary: On 21 April 2026, the EU added Euromore (a pro-Kremlin media platform) and Pravfond (Foundation for the Support and Protection of the Rights of Compatriots Living Abroad) to its sanctions list for ongoing influence operations targeting European audiences. Pravfond is described by the Council of the EU as a core instrument of Russian foreign influence strategy, financed by the Russian state. Euromore amplifies Kremlin narratives attacking the EU and legitimising the war against Ukraine.
campaigns.new_this_week: EUvsDisinfo published a detailed analysis of the sanctions designations in the current monitoring window, providing public documentation of both entities' roles in the Russian FIMI architecture. This is the most recent formal EU infrastructure attribution action.

sources_cited.excerpt: on 21 April 2026, the EU added Euromore and Pravfond to its sanctions list for ongoing influence operations

search_queries_run: EU EUvsDisinfo FIMI state-sponsored disinformation May 2026
research_traceback.confidence_notes: Confirmed: formal Council of the EU sanctions designation constitutes government attribution. Primary source is EU official action, corroborated by EUvsDisinfo analysis. No single-source dependency — Council decision and EUvsDisinfo analysis are independent.

campaigns.target: European political figures; Iranian exile community; Greenland/Arctic narrative space
campaigns.summary: Russian prank-call duo Vladimir Kuznetsov (Vovan) and Alexei Stolyarov (Lexus) conducted operations in March 2026 targeting Greenland's Foreign Minister and Iranian exile Reza Pahlavi. The Greenland call was amplified by the Russian FIMI network Pravda to reinforce narratives that Russia is not a genuine European threat. The Pahlavi call sought to discredit him as coordinating with Western actors on military action against Iran, echoing Moscow-Tehran alignment narratives.
campaigns.new_this_week: EUvsDisinfo published a dedicated analytical piece on the Vovan/Lexus FIMI instrument within the current monitoring window, contextualising the March 2026 operations within the broader Russian FIMI architecture and documenting amplification by state-linked networks.

sources_cited.excerpt: framed as comedy, their work is highly political, consistently reinforcing narratives favourable to Moscow

search_queries_run: EU EUvsDisinfo FIMI state-sponsored disinformation May 2026
research_traceback.confidence_notes: MF2: attribution to Russian state services is assessed, not confirmed — Vovan/Lexus have denied FSB links. MF3: amplification chain via Pravda network is documented by EUvsDisinfo but independent verification of the full distribution network is partial. Assessed confidence is appropriate.

campaigns.operation_name: Russian FIMI — Recycled Narratives on Ukraine, Bucha, Biolabs (Active Cycle)
campaigns.target: European and global audiences — Ukraine support, NATO legitimacy
campaigns.summary: EUvsDisinfo's current monitoring cycle documents active circulation of recycled pro-Kremlin narratives: Ukraine recast as a terrorist state, NATO as aggressor, Bucha massacre denial evolving into conspiracy theories alleging Ukraine is eliminating witnesses, and the 'secret biolabs' narrative resurfacing in new geographic contexts. One variant of the NATO weapons depot narrative was traced to an AI-generated article published by a Chinese outlet, suggesting opportunistic cross-actor amplification.
campaigns.new_this_week: The AI-generated Chinese outlet article feeding into a Russian FIMI narrative about a secret NATO weapons depot in Romania is a newly documented cross-actor amplification instance, flagged in the current EUvsDisinfo monitoring cycle.

sources_cited.excerpt: original news story had been taken from an AI-generated article with no factual basis published by a Chinese outlet

search_queries_run: EU EUvsDisinfo FIMI state-sponsored disinformation May 2026
research_traceback.confidence_notes: MF4 flagged: some source material originates in Russian state media (RT Spanish-language). The cross-actor AI-generated amplification instance is single-source (EUvsDisinfo) but the broader narrative patterns are multiply documented. High confidence on Russian attribution for the narrative cluster; the Chinese outlet's role is Possible/Assessed only.

campaigns.operation_name: Meta H1 2026 — Iranian Covert Influence Operation (Q4 2025 Disruption)
campaigns.summary: Meta's H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (published March 11, 2026) documented the detection and disruption of an Iranian covert influence operation that began targeting English-speaking audiences in summer 2025. Meta detected and disrupted the operation in Q4 2025, before it had time to build an authentic audience. The report identifies Russia, Iran, and China as the three leading sources of foreign influence operations globally.
campaigns.new_this_week: Not new this week — published March 11, 2026. Included for completeness of the Iran actor tracker. No new Meta CIB disclosure was published in the April 28–May 4 window.

sources_cited.source_name: Meta Transparency Center — H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report
sources_cited.excerpt: One Iranian operation began targeting English-speaking audiences during summer of 2025, and we detected and disrupted it during Q4

search_queries_run: Meta CIB coordinated inauthentic behaviour report 2026
research_traceback.confidence_notes: Confirmed platform disclosure. Attribution to Iran is Meta's own assessment. Published March 11, 2026 — not within the current 7-day window but most recent available Tier 1 disclosure for Iran.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: Persistent multi-vector FIMI doctrine: electoral interference via narrative warfare, infrastructure replication across target countries, prank-call social engineering, AI-assisted content production, and cross-platform amplification via state-linked media networks. Moldova playbook now being operationally transferred to Armenia.
actor_tracker.headline: Russia intensifies FIMI operations targeting Armenia ahead of June 2026 elections; EU sanctions Euromore and Pravfond as FIMI infrastructure nodes.
actor_tracker.summary: Russia remains the dominant FIMI actor in the EEAS monitoring perimeter, responsible for 29% of all documented FIMI incidents in 2025. The current week shows active narrative recycling on Ukraine/NATO themes and confirmed infrastructure-level action via the Euromore/Pravfond sanctions designations.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: Persistent large-scale YouTube/Blogger channel network operations (thousands of channels per quarter) focused on China-US foreign affairs narratives and spammy content. Opportunistic amplification of Russian narratives documented (AI-generated article feeding Russian FIMI). EEAS attributes 6% of 2025 FIMI incidents to China.
actor_tracker.headline: Chinese FIMI infrastructure documented in opportunistic amplification of Russian NATO-Romania narrative via AI-generated content.
actor_tracker.summary: China's FIMI posture in the current week is characterised by the newly documented instance of a Chinese outlet's AI-generated article being picked up and amplified within Russian FIMI channels — a pattern the EEAS describes as 'opportunistic amplification.' No new Tier 1 platform disclosure for China was issued this week.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: Iran's most recent documented operation (Meta H1 2026 report) targeted English-speaking audiences with a covert influence operation disrupted before achieving scale. Iran continues to operate across multiple platforms but at lower documented intensity than Russia or China in the current monitoring window.
actor_tracker.headline: No new Iran FIMI disclosures this week; most recent Tier 1 action was Meta's Q4 2025 disruption of an English-language covert operation.
actor_tracker.summary: Iran remains a monitored actor with confirmed covert influence operation capability, but no new Tier 1–3 disclosures were published in the April 28–May 4 window. The structural OSINT gap for Iran on non-Meta platforms remains.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: Structural OSINT gap persists. No Tier 1–3 disclosures for Gulf state FIMI operations were identified in the current monitoring window. Gulf actors are underrepresented in platform transparency reporting relative to their assessed operational capacity.
actor_tracker.headline: No new Gulf state FIMI disclosures identified this week — structural coverage gap applies.
actor_tracker.summary: Gulf state FIMI activity remains below the detection threshold of current Tier 1–3 monitoring infrastructure. Absence of disclosure should not be interpreted as absence of activity. This gap is structural and consistent with prior monitoring cycles.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: No US-attributed FIMI operations identified in Tier 1–3 sources this week. The US State Department Global Engagement Center's operational status under the current administration remains a coverage gap. Applying identical evidentiary standards: no evidence found this week.
actor_tracker.headline: No US-attributed FIMI operations identified in Tier 1–3 sources this week.
actor_tracker.summary: Applying identical evidentiary standards across all six actors: no Tier 1–3 source published evidence of US-attributed FIMI operations in the current monitoring window. The GEC's reduced operational profile under the current administration creates a structural transparency gap for this actor.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin documented termination of 2 YouTube channels and 1 Ads account linked to Israel-linked coordinated influence operations sharing content critical of Canada. No new disclosures this week. Structural OSINT gap for Israeli operations on non-Google platforms.
actor_tracker.headline: No new Israel-attributed FIMI disclosures this week; most recent Tier 1 action was Google TAG Q4 2025 termination of Israel-linked YouTube channels targeting Canada.
actor_tracker.summary: Israel's most recently documented FIMI-adjacent activity (Google TAG Q4 2025) involved a small-scale YouTube operation critical of Canada. No new disclosures were published in the current monitoring window. Confidence remains low due to structural OSINT gaps.

platform_responses.summary: Meta published its H1 2026 Semiannual Adversarial Threat Report, documenting disruption of covert influence operations from Russia, Iran, and China, including an Iranian operation targeting English-speaking audiences disrupted in Q4 2025. Report also covered scam centre account takedowns (10.9 million accounts in 2025). Not published this week — most recent available Tier 1 disclosure.

Google TAG published Q4 2025 bulletin documenting termination of over 1,054 YouTube channels linked to Russia, 2 YouTube channels and 1 Ads account linked to Israel (content critical of Canada), and blocking of 22 domains linked to Russian operations targeting multiple languages. Not published this week — most recent available Tier 1 disclosure.

EU Council imposed sanctions on Euromore (pro-Kremlin media platform) and Pravfond (Russian state-financed foreign influence financing vehicle) for ongoing FIMI operations targeting European audiences. This constitutes a regulatory infrastructure action rather than a platform takedown, but functions as an enforcement action against FIMI infrastructure nodes.

No CIB-equivalent report or transparency disclosure published by X/Twitter this week or in recent months. X/Twitter has no structural equivalent to Meta's CIB reporting or Google TAG's quarterly bulletins. Absence of disclosure is recorded separately from absence of activity — the platform's reduced trust and safety infrastructure since 2022 creates a persistent structural transparency gap.

cognitive_warfare.headline: AI-generated content from Chinese outlet feeds Russian FIMI narrative on NATO Romania weapons depot
cognitive_warfare.detail: EUvsDisinfo documented a case in the current monitoring cycle where a false narrative about a secret NATO weapons depot in Romania — allegedly destroyed by Russian forces killing dozens of Romanian officers — was traced back to an AI-generated article with no factual basis published by a Chinese outlet. The article was then picked up and amplified within Russian FIMI channels. This represents a documented instance of AI-generated synthetic content crossing actor boundaries to serve Russian strategic narratives, without evidence of deliberate coordination between Chinese and Russian operators.
cognitive_warfare.significance: This case illustrates the emerging cognitive warfare risk of AI-generated content pollution: even without deliberate inter-actor coordination, AI-generated false content produced by one actor's ecosystem can be opportunistically harvested by another actor's FIMI infrastructure. This creates attribution complexity and accelerates narrative velocity in ways that outpace traditional fact-checking response cycles. It also demonstrates LLM-era information pollution as a force multiplier for FIMI.

cognitive_warfare.headline: EEAS 4th FIMI Report: AI use in FIMI incidents increased exponentially in 2025; LLMs being 'groomed' via information flooding
cognitive_warfare.detail: The EEAS 4th FIMI Threat Report (March 2026) documents that compared to 2024, the use of AI tools in FIMI incidents increased exponentially in 2025. AI-generated videos and images are described as 'the new norm.' The report also flags a novel LLM manipulation vector: operators flooding the internet with false information in an effort to manipulate LLM training data and query results — a form of long-horizon cognitive infrastructure attack.
cognitive_warfare.significance: The LLM-grooming vector represents a qualitative shift in FIMI doctrine: rather than targeting human audiences directly, operators are now attempting to corrupt the AI systems that mediate human information access. This is a second-order cognitive warfare capability with long-term epistemic consequences that current platform transparency frameworks are not designed to detect or report.

cognitive_warfare.headline: Vovan/Lexus prank-call operations as hybrid social engineering and narrative injection instrument
cognitive_warfare.detail: EUvsDisinfo's analysis of the Vovan/Lexus operations documents their function as a FIMI instrument: calls are framed as comedy but consistently reinforce Kremlin-favourable narratives. The March 2026 Greenland call was amplified by the Pravda FIMI network to advance the narrative that Russia is not a genuine European threat. The Pahlavi call sought to discredit Iranian exile opposition by portraying them as coordinating with Western military actors. The operations exploit the credibility of real interlocutors to generate authentic-seeming content for FIMI amplification.
cognitive_warfare.significance: Prank-call operations represent a low-cost, high-credibility social engineering vector that exploits the reputational capital of legitimate political figures. The content generated is technically authentic (real people, real voices) while the framing and distribution are manipulative — creating a category of FIMI that is resistant to standard content-based detection and fact-checking.

attribution_log.headline: EU Council formally designates Euromore and Pravfond as Russian FIMI infrastructure, imposing sanctions.
attribution_log.summary: The Council of the EU formally added Euromore and Pravfond to its sanctions list on 21 April 2026. Pravfond is described as a core instrument of Russian foreign influence strategy, founded and financed by the Russian state, serving as a financing channel for pro-Kremlin actors across European countries. Euromore is identified as operating within the pro-Kremlin information architecture to amplify narratives attacking the EU and legitimising the war against Ukraine. This constitutes formal government-level attribution with legal standing.

attribution_log.headline: Meta H1 2026 report attributes increasing sophistication in covert influence operations to Russia, Iran, and China.
attribution_log.summary: Meta's H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report identifies Russia, Iran, and China as the three leading sources of foreign influence operations globally, noting increasing sophistication and tradecraft. An Iranian operation targeting English-speaking audiences was disrupted in Q4 2025 before achieving audience scale. Attribution is Meta's own platform-level assessment based on behavioural and infrastructure analysis.

attribution_log.headline: Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin documents Israel-linked coordinated influence operation targeting Canada via YouTube.
attribution_log.summary: Google TAG's Q4 2025 bulletin documents the termination of 2 YouTube channels and 1 Ads account linked to coordinated influence operations attributed to Israel, sharing content in English critical of Canada. Attribution is Google's platform-level assessment. No corroborating Tier 2–3 source was identified for this specific operation. MF2 applies: single-source attribution.

cross_monitor_signals.wdm: Armenia's June 2026 parliamentary elections are now an active FIMI target, with Russian operations documented replicating the Moldova 2025 playbook. This represents a direct threat to democratic integrity in a country navigating a geopolitical pivot toward the EU. The EEAS explicitly flags Armenia as a priority target for the current cycle.
cross_monitor_signals.scem: Russian FIMI operations continue to run in parallel with the ongoing Ukraine conflict, with active narrative warfare targeting NATO legitimacy, Bucha massacre denial, and fabricated claims about Western military infrastructure. The AI-generated Chinese outlet article feeding a Russian narrative about Romanian NATO casualties illustrates how information operations are being used to shape perceptions of conflict escalation risk.
cross_monitor_signals.esa: Multiple active FIMI vectors targeting EU institutions and integration this week: (1) Euromore/Pravfond sanctions designations confirm ongoing Russian operations targeting EU legitimacy; (2) EUvsDisinfo documents active narratives recasting EU-Armenia cooperation as 'foreign interference'; (3) recycled 'biolabs' and NATO-aggressor narratives continue to circulate targeting EU member state audiences. The European Democracy Shield framework is referenced as the primary EU counter-FIMI structural response.
cross_monitor_signals.agm: Two significant AI-in-FIMI signals this week: (1) EEAS 4th Report documents exponential increase in AI tool use in FIMI incidents in 2025, with AI-generated video and images now described as 'the new norm'; (2) novel LLM-grooming vector documented — operators flooding internet with false content to manipulate LLM training data and query outputs; (3) specific case of AI-generated Chinese outlet article feeding Russian FIMI narrative chain documented by EUvsDisinfo.

weekly_brief_narrative: Week ending 9 May 2026 presents a moderate-signal environment with no new Tier 1 platform disclosures (Meta CIB, Google TAG, MSTIC) published within the seven-day window. The most analytically significant development is the active operationalisation of Russia's Moldova FIMI playbook against Armenia ahead of its June 2026 parliamentary elections. The EEAS 4th FIMI Threat Report (March 2026) and EUvsDisinfo's live monitoring both document network and tactical similarities between the Moldova 2025 and Armenia 2026 operations, including fear-spreading, smear campaigns against pro-European leaders, and the framing of EU-Armenia cooperation as illegitimate foreign interference. This is not a new campaign in the abstract sense — it is a documented infrastructure transfer with forward-looking operational significance. The EU's April 21 sanctions designations of Euromore and Pravfond represent the most recent formal attribution action, confirming two nodes in the Russian FIMI financing and amplification architecture. Pravfond's designation as a state-financed channel for pro-Kremlin actors across European countries is particularly significant for infrastructure mapping. On the AI-in-FIMI front, two signals warrant analyst attention. First, the EEAS documents an exponential increase in AI tool use in FIMI incidents across 2025, with AI-generated video and images now described as operationally normalised. Second, a novel LLM-grooming vector is documented: operators deliberately flooding the internet with false content to corrupt LLM training data and query outputs — a second-order cognitive infrastructure attack that current platform transparency frameworks are not designed to detect. A specific instance of AI-generated content crossing actor boundaries — a Chinese outlet's fabricated article about a NATO weapons depot in Romania being amplified by Russian FIMI channels — illustrates the attribution complexity this creates. Platform transparency this week is structurally thin. No new Meta CIB report, Google TAG bulletin, or MSTIC disclosure was published in the monitoring window. The most recent Tier 1 disclosures remain Meta's H1 2026 report (March 11) and Google TAG Q4 2025 (January 29). X/Twitter continues to produce no CIB-equivalent disclosure; this structural absence is recorded separately from absence of activity. Notable absences: no Gulf state, US, or new Israeli FIMI disclosures were identified. These gaps are structural and consistent with prior cycles — they reflect monitoring infrastructure limitations, not confirmed operational quiet. The Armenia election window (June 2026) is the primary near-term FIMI risk horizon in the EU neighbourhood.

research_coverage.x_twitter_gap_note: X/Twitter has no CIB-equivalent reporting mechanism and published no transparency disclosures relevant to FIMI in the current monitoring window. This is a structural absence, not an operational one. The platform's reduced trust and safety infrastructure since 2022 means that influence operations migrating to or operating on X/Twitter are systematically underreported in Tier 1 disclosures. Stanford Internet Observatory research (2024) documented that accounts linked to Russian and Chinese networks identified by Meta remained active on X after Meta's takedowns, suggesting ongoing activity not captured in any current disclosure framework.

open_findings.headline: EEAS 4th FIMI Threat Report documents LLM-grooming as an emerging FIMI vector: operators flooding the internet with false content to manipulate AI training data and query outputs.
open_findings.why_it_matters: This represents a qualitative escalation in FIMI doctrine — from targeting human cognition directly to corrupting the AI intermediaries through which humans increasingly access information, creating long-horizon epistemic infrastructure attacks that no current platform transparency or regulatory framework is designed to detect or counter.

open_findings.headline: EU DisinfoLab monitoring templates for FIMI incidents under DSA Article 40 obligations are now operational, providing a structured framework for systemic infringement assessment.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The operationalisation of DSA-linked FIMI monitoring templates by EU DisinfoLab signals a shift from voluntary platform transparency toward regulatory-compelled disclosure infrastructure — a structural change in the European FIMI accountability architecture that will affect how Tier 1 disclosures are generated and assessed in future monitoring cycles.

open_findings.headline: EUvsDisinfo FIMI Galaxy Explorer — first public interactive dashboard on FIMI activities — is now live, enabling open-source cross-campaign infrastructure mapping.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The public availability of an interactive FIMI incident dashboard lowers the barrier to OSINT-level infrastructure attribution and cross-campaign pattern analysis, potentially accelerating the detection cycle for new operations — but also creating a public signal that sophisticated FIMI operators can use to calibrate evasion.

open_findings.headline: Russia's FIMI funding is set to rise in 2026 as part of a budget increase for state-controlled media, per EEAS 4th Threat Report assessment.
open_findings.why_it_matters: A documented increase in FIMI resourcing — not just tactical adaptation — signals a strategic commitment to information warfare as a durable instrument of Russian foreign policy, with direct implications for the scale and persistence of operations targeting EU elections and neighbourhood countries through 2026 and beyond.

open_findings.headline: World Economic Forum Global Risks Report 2026 ranks misinformation and disinformation as the second most serious global short-term risk, down from first in 2025 — a potential signal of risk normalisation.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The marginal downward shift in WEF ranking may reflect risk normalisation rather than genuine threat reduction — a cognitive dynamic that FIMI operators can exploit by sustaining operations below the threshold of acute public concern while achieving cumulative epistemic effects.
